case_name: Similar views have been expressed during the Pandemic, including by Johnson J inSC v Southampton NHS[2020] EWHC 1445 (QB), a clinical negligence case (although that was the height of the first lockdown when the issue was attendance at court generally, which he found could be arranged).SCwas cited very recently in allowing a witness with an underlying health condition not to travel to the UK to give evidence on an allegation of forgery:Rahbarpour v Said[2021] 11 WLUK 159.DHCJ Ambrose there rightly observed that whether to permit evidence remotely was a matter for the court's discretion in the light of the overriding objective, fairness and efficiency, and the need for equality of arms between the parties under ECHR art.6.

summary.brief: Similar views have been expressed during the Pandemic, including by Johnson J inSC v Southampton NHS[2020] EWHC 1445 (QB), a clinical negligence case (although that was the height of the first lockdown when the issue was attendance at court generally, which he found could be arranged).SCwas cited very recently in allowing a witness with an underlying health condition not to travel to the UK to give evidence on an allegation of forgery:Rahbarpour v Said[2021] 11 WLUK 159.DHCJ Ambrose there rightl

evidence_index.text: Callum Best was born on 27 June 2002. Just before his birth he suffered a severe brain injury from being deprived of oxygen because his placenta became detached from the wall of the uterus of his mum, the Claimant (a 'placental abruption').
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Their pleaded case is that on the morning of his birth, having had an ante-natal appointment at 36 weeks' pregnancy which went well, at about 10.30am when they were out shopping the Claimant suddenly experienced sudden intense abdominal pain.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The parties' Midwifery Experts Angela Cook for the Claimant and Lynn Smith for the Defendant agree that it would have been negligent for a midwife, if told the Claimant was in sudden intense abdominal pain, to fail to instruct her to come in immediately.
evidence_index.speaker: Midwifery Experts

evidence_index.text: The parties' obstetric experts Mr Duthie for the Claimant and Mr Tuffnell for the Defendant also agree that if there was such a phone call and the Claimant had been told to come in immediately, Callum would probably have been delivered within an hour of attendance: around 11.40 or so.
evidence_index.speaker: Obstetric Experts

evidence_index.text: I therefore uphold that claim and award the Claimant the agreed sum of £500,000 but perhaps more importantly for them, vindication of what they said happened to Callum.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The Bolitho question then arises about what would have happened had that question been asked as I find it should have been.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: The Claimant's evidence over CVP in Court was manageable but there were issues with echo etc which required patience from Mr Feeny, for which I am grateful.
evidence_index.speaker: Court observation

evidence_index.text: By the time they got to the car, the Claimant's pain was worse still.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: As the Claimant sat in the car in increasing pain, doubled over again as Mr Orton recalled, he was understandably panicking about what the Claimant was telling him about fearing losing the baby, called the number in the Midwifery Notes.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: I simply find that if that call had been recorded, then the identity of the midwife I find took it would be known and could be called to give evidence about it.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: The patient, 36 weeks pregnant, experienced sudden intense abdominal pain while shopping. Her partner called the maternity unit and was allegedly advised to take her home and give her paracetamol.

requestables.name: Ultrasound
requestables.description: Ultrasound to assess fetal well-being and placental status

reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt immediate delivery if abruption confirmed

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient experiences sudden intense abdominal pain while shopping.

key_events.event: Patient experiences pain and partner calls maternity unit

timeline_phases.name: Hospital Admission
timeline_phases.description: Patient admitted to hospital after worsening symptoms.

key_events.event: Patient arrives at A&E and is transferred to Maternity Unit

timeline_phases.name: Decision Making
timeline_phases.description: Midwife receives call about patient's severe abdominal pain.

key_events.event: Midwife advises patient to go home and take paracetamol

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient reports sudden intense abdominal pain at 36 weeks gestation
decision_points.prompt: What should be the immediate advice given to the patient?

options.description: Advise immediate hospital admission for assessment
options.clinical_reasoning: Intense abdominal pain in late pregnancy is a red flag for placental abruption.

options.description: Advise rest at home and take paracetamol
options.clinical_reasoning: Inappropriate given the severity of symptoms and gestational age.

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant allegedly advised rest at home and paracetamol
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant denies call was made, but if it was, advice was inappropriate.

expected_action_court.description: Immediate hospital admission should have been advised
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert consensus that intense pain requires urgent assessment

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to advise immediate admission for suspected placental abruption.
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Patient should have been advised to come to the hospital immediately.

patient_outcome.description: Patient's condition worsened, leading to severe brain injury in the newborn.
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Severe disabilities in the child, leading to significant care needs

key_findings: Midwife failed to adequately assess the severity of the patient's pain.
Causation established - earlier intervention could have prevented the newborn's brain injury.

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to escalate care upon report of severe pain
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Immediate hospital visit could have prevented the adverse outcome

factual_timeline.event: Patient experiences sudden intense abdominal pain
Patient arrives at A&E and is transferred to Maternity Unit
Midwife advises patient to go home and take paracetamol

treatments.outcome: Callum born with severe brain injury

allegations.allegation: Failure to advise immediate hospital admission
Failure to adequately assess and escalate care for severe abdominal pain

court_findings.finding: Midwife breached duty of care by not assessing pain severity
